topics.title: MySQL Indexing — Clustered, Composite & Covering Indexes
topics.description: An index is a B-Tree data structure that allows MySQL to find rows in O(log n) instead of O(n) full table scan. InnoDB has a clustered index (primary key = data itself). Secondary indexes store the PK as the row locator. Composite indexes follow the leftmost prefix rule. A covering index satisfies a query entirely from the index — no table lookup needed. Incorrect indexing is the #1 cause of slow queries in production.
topics.story: A table without an index is like a book without a table of contents — every search reads every page (full table scan). An index is the table of contents: find the page number fast, then flip directly to it. The clustered index is even better — the data IS arranged in page order (primary key order). A covering index is like the table of contents containing the full paragraph — you never need to open the main book at all.
topics.analogy: Clustered index = phone book sorted by last name (data physically ordered). Secondary index = index at the back of a textbook (word → page number to look up the content). Composite index = multi-column sort: sorted by (last_name, first_name) — 'last_name = Smith' works, 'first_name = John' alone doesn't use the index. Covering index = the index has all columns the query needs — never touches the actual rows. Index scan = jumping around the B-Tree. Full table scan = reading every page.
topics.memoryAnchor: Clustered index = PK = data row order in InnoDB. Secondary index leaf = PK value (requires second lookup). Composite: (a,b,c) → WHERE a=? uses index. WHERE b=? skips index. WHERE a=? AND b=? uses both. Covering: SELECT cols in index — no heap fetch. EXPLAIN key/key_len/Extra='Using index'. Cardinality: high = good for index (user_id). Low = bad (gender, boolean). Index on FK columns always. Prefix index: INDEX(email(20)). Invisible index (MySQL 8): test drop without removing.
topics.firstPrinciples: B-Tree index: balanced tree with O(log n) search. Leaf nodes contain data pointers. InnoDB clustered index stores actual row data at leaf — secondary index lookups do two B-Tree traversals (index + PK). Composite index selectivity: (a,b) index sorts by a first, then b within same a — queries on b alone can't use the index efficiently.
topics.bruteForce: No index: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id=42 → full table scan reading all rows. For 10M rows: reads 10M rows, returns a few. Index on customer_id: finds matching rows in O(log n) + return matching count. For 10M rows with 1000 matches: reads ~20 B-Tree nodes + 1000 rows = 1020 reads vs 10,000,000.
topics.optimizedApproach: Index strategy: index columns in WHERE, JOIN ON, ORDER BY, GROUP BY. Composite index order: highest selectivity first, or match most common query pattern. Covering index: include projected columns with INCLUDE() or in composite. EXPLAIN to verify: key≠NULL, rows≈actual matches, Extra has no 'Using filesort'/'Using temporary'.
topics.whenToUse: Add index: FK columns (always), high-cardinality WHERE columns, JOIN columns. Avoid: low-cardinality columns (status, gender), columns rarely queried, tables with heavy write (index overhead). Composite vs multiple: composite serves range queries on prefix; multiple single-column indexes can't combine for range. DROP unused indexes (slow writes, waste space). Index hints only as last resort.

examples.title: EXPLAIN — Reading Query Execution Plans
examples.code:
-- Setup
CREATE TABLE orders (
    id          BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    customer_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
    status      VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    amount      DECIMAL(10,2),
    created_at  DATETIME NOT NULL
);

-- Bad: full table scan
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'PENDING';
-- type: ALL, key: NULL, rows: ~10000000 ← scanning everything

-- Add targeted index
CREATE INDEX idx_status ON orders(status);
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'PENDING';
-- type: ref, key: idx_status, rows: ~50000
-- BUT status has low cardinality — may still do full scan for large %

-- Better: composite index for common query pattern
CREATE INDEX idx_customer_status ON orders(customer_id, status);
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders
    WHERE customer_id = 42 AND status = 'PENDING'
    ORDER BY created_at DESC;
-- Uses idx_customer_status for WHERE, but filesort for ORDER BY

-- Best: covering index for this query
DROP INDEX idx_customer_status ON orders;
CREATE INDEX idx_cov ON orders(customer_id, status, created_at, amount);
EXPLAIN SELECT amount, created_at FROM orders
    WHERE customer_id = 42 AND status = 'PENDING'
    ORDER BY created_at DESC;
-- Extra: Using index (covering!) — zero heap access
-- type: range, key: idx_cov, rows: ~100

-- Leftmost prefix rule demo
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'PENDING'; -- no customer_id in WHERE
-- idx_cov NOT used (status is not leftmost column)
-- Need separate index for status-only queries
examples.explanation: EXPLAIN output key fields: type (ALL=bad, range/ref=good, const=best), key (which index used), rows (estimated rows scanned), Extra ('Using index'=covering, 'Using filesort'=needs sort, 'Using temporary'=very bad). The composite index idx_cov covers customer_id+status+created_at+amount — query with those columns never touches the table rows.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner submissions table: INDEX(user_id, topic_id, status) for 'show all accepted submissions for user+topic'. Topics: INDEX(category, display_order) for sidebar query. Problems: INDEX(topic_id, difficulty) for gate queries. Always run EXPLAIN before deploying a query that touches large tables.

examples.title: Composite Index Design & Common Mistakes
examples.code:
-- SETUP
CREATE TABLE employees (
    id         INT PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_id    INT,
    salary     INT,
    hire_date  DATE,
    status     VARCHAR(10)
);

-- ── Leftmost prefix rule ──────────────────────────────────────
CREATE INDEX idx_dept_salary_date ON employees(dept_id, salary, hire_date);

-- Uses index: dept_id is leftmost ✓
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 5;              -- ✓
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 5 AND salary > 50000; -- ✓
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 5 AND salary = 60000 AND hire_date > '2020-01-01'; -- ✓

-- Does NOT use index: skipping leftmost column ✗
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary > 50000;           -- ✗ full scan
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM employees WHERE hire_date > '2020-01-01'; -- ✗ full scan

-- Stops at range column: range on salary breaks next columns
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM employees
    WHERE dept_id = 5 AND salary > 50000 AND hire_date > '2020-01-01';
-- Uses: dept_id ✓ salary (range) ✓ hire_date ✗ (can't use after range)

-- ── Common mistake: function on indexed column ───────────────
-- BAD: function prevents index use
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE YEAR(hire_date) = 2023; -- full scan!
-- GOOD: range on column directly
SELECT * FROM employees
    WHERE hire_date >= '2023-01-01' AND hire_date < '2024-01-01'; -- uses index

-- ── Implicit type conversion breaks index ─────────────────────
-- BAD: id is INT, comparing with string → implicit cast → full scan
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id = '5'; -- type mismatch → no index!
-- GOOD:
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id = 5;  -- uses PK index

-- ── Index for ORDER BY / GROUP BY ────────────────────────────
-- If index matches ORDER BY order: avoids filesort
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE dept_id = 5 ORDER BY salary;
-- idx_dept_salary_date handles this perfectly — sorted by salary within dept
examples.explanation: The leftmost prefix rule: composite index (a,b,c) can be used for queries on (a), (a,b), or (a,b,c) — never for (b) or (c) alone or (b,c). Range conditions stop prefix usage for subsequent columns. Functions on indexed columns destroy index usage — always rewrite as range on the column. Type mismatch causes implicit conversion which prevents index use.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: 'submissions by user, filtered by status, ordered by date' → INDEX(user_id, status, created_at). Never do WHERE LOWER(email)='...' — store email as lowercase. Never DATE(created_at)='2024-01-15' — use range. FK columns (topic_id, user_id) always need indexes in MySQL (InnoDB doesn't auto-create them on FK references).

problems.title: Identify Missing Indexes
problems.description:
Given these slow queries on a `products` table (id, name, category, price, seller_id, created_at), identify which indexes to create for each:
1. WHERE category='electronics' ORDER BY price ASC
2. WHERE seller_id=42 AND price < 100
3. SELECT name, price WHERE category='books' (covering)
4. WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01'
problems.hint1: ORDER BY column should be in the index to avoid filesort.
problems.hint2: Covering index: all SELECT columns must be in the index.
problems.hint3: Equality columns before range columns in composite index.
problems.starterCode:
-- For each query, write the CREATE INDEX statement:

-- Query 1: WHERE category='electronics' ORDER BY price ASC
-- Answer:

-- Query 2: WHERE seller_id=42 AND price < 100
-- Answer:

-- Query 3: SELECT name, price WHERE category='books' (covering index)
-- Answer:

-- Query 4: WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01'
-- Answer:
problems.testCases:
INDEX(category, price)
INDEX(seller_id, price)
INDEX(category, name, price)
INDEX(created_at)

problems.title: Index-Breaking Query Rewrites
problems.description:
Each query below breaks index usage. Rewrite to enable the index:
1. WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024 (index on created_at)
2. WHERE LOWER(email) = 'alice@example.com' (index on email)
3. WHERE id + 1 = 43 (index on id)
4. WHERE status != 'CANCELLED' (index on status)
problems.hint1: Replace functions with range conditions on the raw column.
problems.hint2: LOWER: store emails lowercase, compare as-is. Or use case-insensitive collation.
problems.hint3: id+1=43 → id=42. status!='X' has low cardinality — index unlikely to help anyway.
problems.starterCode:
-- Rewrite each query to be index-friendly:

-- 1. YEAR(created_at) = 2024
-- Rewrite:

-- 2. LOWER(email) = 'alice@example.com'
-- Rewrite:

-- 3. id + 1 = 43
-- Rewrite:

-- 4. status != 'CANCELLED'
-- Note about this one:
problems.solutionCode:
-- 1. Range on column (no function)
WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2025-01-01';
-- Uses index on created_at ✓

-- 2. Store email lowercase in DB; compare directly
WHERE email = 'alice@example.com';
-- (Or use email VARCHAR COLLATE utf8mb4_general_ci for case-insensitive)
-- Uses index on email ✓

-- 3. Move constant to other side
WHERE id = 42;
-- Uses primary key index ✓

-- 4. status != 'CANCELLED'
-- Low-cardinality column — index rarely helps for NOT equals
-- If needed: rewrite as IN list of valid statuses
WHERE status IN ('PENDING', 'PROCESSING', 'COMPLETED');
-- May use index if low % of rows match
problems.testCases: All 4 rewrites avoid functions on indexed columns

problems.title: Design Indexes for E-Commerce Queries
problems.description:
Design indexes for an `orders` table (id, user_id, product_id, status, amount, created_at) to optimize:
1. User's order history (user orders sorted by date)
2. Admin dashboard (pending orders sorted by amount desc)
3. Monthly revenue report (sum of amounts in date range)
4. Product popularity (count of orders per product)
problems.hint1: Query 1: WHERE user_id + ORDER BY created_at → composite (user_id, created_at).
problems.hint2: Query 2: WHERE status='pending' + ORDER BY amount → (status, amount).
problems.hint3: Query 3: range on created_at, aggregate → just created_at index. Query 4: FK index on product_id.
problems.starterCode:
-- Design indexes for all 4 query patterns:
-- orders(id PK, user_id, product_id, status, amount, created_at)

-- Query 1: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id=? ORDER BY created_at DESC

-- Query 2: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status='PENDING' ORDER BY amount DESC LIMIT 100

-- Query 3: SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE created_at BETWEEN ? AND ?

-- Query 4: SELECT product_id, COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY product_id
problems.solutionCode:
-- Query 1: user orders by date
CREATE INDEX idx_user_date ON orders(user_id, created_at DESC);
-- Covers WHERE user_id + ORDER BY created_at in one index

-- Query 2: admin pending orders
CREATE INDEX idx_status_amount ON orders(status, amount DESC);
-- WHERE status='PENDING' → index range, already sorted by amount

-- Query 3: revenue range query
CREATE INDEX idx_date_amount ON orders(created_at, amount);
-- Range on created_at, covering amount for SUM → no heap

-- Query 4: product grouping
CREATE INDEX idx_product ON orders(product_id);
-- Allows GROUP BY product_id using index instead of full scan

-- Summary: 4 queries → 4 targeted indexes
-- Trade-off: each index slows INSERT/UPDATE slightly → acceptable for read-heavy reporting
problems.testCases:
INDEX(user_id, created_at)
INDEX(status, amount)
INDEX(created_at, amount)
INDEX(product_id)
